title: Low-power communications schedule
summary: A schedule for keeping family, helpers, and neighbors informed without draining batteries or spreading rumors.
content: In disruptions, communication fails technically and socially. Phones die, networks overload, and rumors fill the silence. A low-power schedule sets exact windows for checking messages, sending status, listening to official updates, and posting verified local information. Everyone should know that silence between windows does not mean abandonment. Messages should be short: location, safe or unsafe, urgent needs, next check-in time. One outside contact can relay updates when local networks are unstable. A paper notice board can answer repeated local questions without every person spending battery.
materials: Phone and charger. Context: “Low-power communications schedule”.
steps: Choose fixed check-in windows such as morning, afternoon, and evening.
Send short status messages with location, status, needs, and next check time.
Turn off unnecessary radios, GPS, Bluetooth, and background apps.
Keep one phone more charged for emergency calls.
Use a paper board for verified local information.
warnings: Do not publish private medical, security, or location details publicly unless safety requires it.
Do not rely only on internet apps.
Rumors can create dangerous crowd movement.
common_mistakes: Keeping screens bright for repeated updates.
Calling everyone separately instead of using one relay.
Letting group chats become rumor engines.
alternatives: Run a smaller version of Low-power communications schedule with one responsible person, one helper, and one visible paper note.
Use a safer fallback location or method for Low-power communications schedule if the first choice becomes wet, crowded, contaminated, dark, unstable, or unsafe.
Pause Low-power communications schedule and verify the written record with a second person before moving scarce supplies.
failure_signs: People cannot explain who is responsible for Low-power communications schedule or when the next check happens.
Supplies connected to Low-power communications schedule are unlabelled, damp, contaminated, damaged, missing, or being used for the wrong purpose.

The routine ignores fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation and starts creating new risk instead of reducing existing risk during “Low-power communications schedule”
when_not_to_use: Do not use Low-power communications schedule as a reason to delay evacuation, emergency calls, medical care, fire response, or official local instructions.
Do not use Low-power communications schedule when the location is structurally unsafe, chemically suspicious, smoky, flooded, violent, or otherwise immediately dangerous.
Do not improvise hazardous repairs, dosing, chemical handling, weapon use, or electrical/gas work under the label of Low-power communications schedule.
short_term: For the first hours of Low-power communications schedule, keep the action small, visible, and reversible: write the goal, assign one owner, protect vulnerable people, separate safe from unsafe supplies, and set a review time.
long_term: If Low-power communications schedule remains necessary for days or weeks, rotate roles, preserve written lessons, replace fragile supplies, and train another person so the group does not depend on one expert.
if_method_fails: If Low-power communications schedule fails, stop escalation, move to the safest fallback, record what failed, protect people first, and restart only with a simpler written plan.
environment_notes: Adapt Low-power communications schedule to the real environment: check fire risk, carbon monoxide risk, tool condition, batteries, and safe isolation, local weather, building condition, water access, light, noise, animals, neighbours, and safe exits before repeating the routine.
